topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a dropped stone. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I stared at the screen, the words mocking me: "Congratulations, [Wrong Name]." My fingers hovered over the 'reply' button, trembling slightly. This wasn't just a mistake; it felt like a calculated intrusion, a deliberate sabotage of my work. I reread the original submission, the carefully crafted arguments suddenly feeling flimsy, exposed. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my office, the floorboards groaning under my quick steps. Every shadow in the room seemed to shift, and I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting someone to materialize and confront me. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I found the notification tucked away in a forgotten corner of my secondary account. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. When I finally opened the file, the shock was immediate, followed by a wave of nausea. It was my piece, the one I’d poured months into, but the byline belonged to a complete stranger. My hands started to shake uncontrollably as I scrolled back through the publication details. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the reality. A sharp, metallic taste flooded my mouth. I walked to the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling intensely vulnerable, as if every gaze in the street was aimed directly at me. I needed to confirm this, needed to know who had done this, but the sheer anxiety paralyzed my ability to type a single question.

[story 3]

The moment I saw the attribution, the air seemed to thin out. A sudden, intense pressure built behind my eyes, making the room feel too bright, too loud. I didn't move for a full minute, just standing there, rooted to the spot, my jaw locked so tightly my molars ached. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. Slowly, deliberately, I picked up the printout, the paper feeling flimsy and unreal in my grasp. My gaze swept over the text, searching for any anomaly, any sign that this was a prank or a serious error. A low, involuntary hum escaped my throat. I started pacing the length of the room, a restless energy vibrating through my limbs, my focus fragmented, unable to settle on a single thought. The world seemed tilted, unstable.
